name: Kaushik Parthasarathy
title: Software Engineer | Agentic AI Developer | Full-Stack & Distributed Systems
bio:
I build software systems end to end, from architecture and algorithms to the thing that's actually running in production. My work spans agentic LLM systems, full stack platforms, distributed backend services, and ML pipelines, with a focus on modular architecture that stays maintainable as it scales and fails gracefully when a piece of it doesn't.

I break complex problems down into their simplest parts, then prioritize from there. Before I build anything, I think about who it's actually for. I see it through their lens, figure out what they actually need, and find the best way to build it. The goal is to execute their vision, not mine. That means iterating off real feedback instead of running with my own take, and learning from what goes wrong instead of repeating it.

Someone technical should be able to pick up what I build and use it. Someone with no background in the domain should be able to poke around and figure it out just from how it's laid out. If it only makes sense to the person who built it, it's not finished. I also treat load as part of the design, not an afterthought. Atlas, a distributed dispatch system, taught me more from the bugs that only showed up under chaos testing at real scale than from anything that worked on the first try.

Previously, I worked on fintech systems at Wells Fargo supporting portfolio applications managing $400B+ in assets, and on computer vision data pipelines developed in collaboration with ISRO and NIT Trichy. I'm currently pursuing an MS in Computer Science at UMass Amherst, focused on AI/ML systems and scalable software engineering.
workAuthorization: F-1 · CPT authorized · Will require STEM OPT / H-1B sponsorship
tagline: Software Engineer focused on building systems that scale and stay maintainable. Full-stack platforms, agentic AI systems, ML Pipelines, distributed backend services, and architectures designed for the real world.

principles: Scalable systems should be boring in production
Deployment is part of the feature
Good architecture makes the next change easier
